strategy: Preserve the transcript-grounded target ingredient, then verify full-video visual coverage by searching for all lettuce/salad appearances and counting only distinct appearances where a cutting board is visible in the same shot.

steps.purpose: Confirm from preprocessing transcripts which ingredient is insulted the most, preserving the prior grounded referent before visual counting.

inputs.query: Using only these transcripts, identify the ingredient insulted the most. Quote the exact insulting line(s), explain why competing ingredient mentions are not stronger insults, and output the canonical visual referent to search for in frames (e.g., lettuce/salad/shredded lettuce).

segments.text: Today we're making smash burgers which are a super violent burger with a very destructive taste. We're going to start by taking out some unsmashed beef. You can buy the pre-smashed stuff if you want but it won't taste nearly as wrecked. We've got a pound here with around 20% fat. You can use the super lean stuff if you want but it might cause you to lose too much weight so be very careful. We'll divide this into six even-ish beef balls.

Hey, your beef. Start acting like it. As far as toppings go, I'm just gonna go with what I have here. Nothing fancy. I've got a nice dank onion, some nice hood tomatoes. Let's just check inside here and hopefully, oh yeah, pickles. That's awesome. To me, this is essential. And then we'll just peel off a couple slices of cupboard cheese.

I think that's it, but let's just check and see if there's any, oh, okay. Hey, listen, this is kind of hard to say, but people are just using you when they put you on a burger to try to make it seem more healthy by including salad. All you really do is get soggy and you taste like nothing. So I just wanted you to know the truth.

Okay great. We're gonna make fresh potato buns which we'll do by taking a cup of flour, half tablespoon of sugar, one teaspoon of instant yeast, half teaspoon of salt, two cups of water, and two potatoes. Then we'll grab those potatoes and just smash them against the wall. If you do it right you'll get this nice crack right in the middle of the buns.

Oh look, it's like a miniature man tried to run through a cartoon wall. If you slice onions thin enough, they won't make you cry, provided you also have no tear ducts. And we'll slice up these magical flavor strips. Do not make a burger without them. If you don't like pickles, one thing you can do is seek help. Then we'll slice up the tomato. Okay, crispy water, you can join this once, but don't get cocky.

Now before we start cooking, we'll do a final check to make sure we have everything, which is known as mise en place, which is a French term for get all your together before you start. I've got the buns here, I've got my beef balls, I've got my sliced pickle ready to go, I've got the onions here, and I've got that shredded crispy water, sliced tomato, and I've got my cheese. So it looks like we're ready.

expected_outputs.answer: confirmed insulted ingredient and canonical visual referent terms
expected_outputs.supporting_points: quoted transcript support for the target ingredient

steps.purpose: Find all candidate intervals across the full video where the target ingredient appears visually, especially lettuce/head of lettuce/shredded lettuce during prep and assembly.

inputs.query: all moments in the full video where lettuce or salad (including head of lettuce, shredded lettuce, crispy water on the burger, or lettuce being cut/prepared/assembled) is visible; prioritize exhaustive recall across the whole video

expected_outputs.clips: candidate full-video intervals where the insulted ingredient may be visible

steps.purpose: Retrieve chronological representative frames from each candidate interval so the final step can verify both ingredient presence and cutting-board presence and deduplicate continuous shots into distinct appearances.

inputs.query: chronological representative frames showing the lettuce/salad appearance and enough surrounding context to tell whether a cutting board is visible in the same shot
time_hints: 0-351s full-video coverage; preserve separate candidate appearances rather than only top-ranked frames

expected_outputs.frames: chronological candidate frames spanning all grounded lettuce/salad appearances

steps.purpose: From the transcript-grounded referent and the full set of candidate frames, verify every distinct appearance where the insulted ingredient is visible with a cutting board in the background, reject non-qualifying appearances, deduplicate continuous runs, and map the final count to an option.

inputs.query: The insulted ingredient has already been identified from transcript evidence. Using the frames, count how many distinct appearances in the full video show that ingredient visibly present together with a cutting board in the same shot/background. Treat continuous neighboring frames from one uninterrupted shot as one appearance, but count temporally separate reappearances as separate appearances. Explicitly list qualifying and rejected appearances with timestamps, explain whether 107-110s is one continuous appearance or multiple, check for any additional qualifying appearances outside the previously sampled windows, and map the final total to exactly one option from: A=1, B=4, C=5, D=2, E=3.

expected_outputs.supporting_points: timestamped qualifying and rejected appearances with deduplicated count
expected_outputs.analysis: reasoning about full-video coverage and distinct-appearance grouping

refinement_instructions: Preserve the prior grounded referent that the insulted ingredient is salad/lettuce, but do not preserve the prior incomplete count as final. The new trace must explicitly state that the earlier 107-110s frames represent one continuous late appearance unless the new full-video evidence shows otherwise. Use step 2+3 outputs to establish full-video coverage for candidate lettuce/salad appearances, then in step 4 count only distinct appearances where both the ingredient and a cutting board are visible in the same shot. Reject shots with only a cutting board, only lettuce without a cutting board, or burger shots where lettuce is not visibly identifiable as the counted ingredient under the question’s intended referent. The final trace must include the exact timestamps of each counted appearance, note any non-qualifying candidate intervals, and end with one selected option.
